questions.question:
Two girls have picked 10 roses, 15 sunflowers and 15 daffodils. What is the number of ways they can divide the flowers among themselves?

A. 1638
B. 2100
C. 2640
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Number of ways two girls can divide 10 roses, 15 sunflowers, and 15 daffodils among themselves

understand.plain: Each flower type is treated as indistinguishable among itself. For each type, girl 1 can receive anywhere from 0 to the total count, with girl 2 receiving the remainder. The total number of ways is the product of the number of choices for each flower type. The computed value does not match any of the given options.

keywords.explain: When n identical objects are split between 2 people, there are n+1 ways (one person can receive 0, 1, 2, ..., or n objects).
keywords.example: Dividing 3 identical balls between 2 people: 4 ways (0|3, 1|2, 2|1, 3|0).

keywords.explain: When independent choices are made for separate groups, the total number of outcomes is the product of the individual counts.
keywords.example: 2 choices for shirts and 3 for pants give 2 x 3 = 6 outfits.

understand.visual_alt: A table showing three rows for roses, sunflowers, and daffodils, each with the number of distribution options.

given.aim: Find the total number of ways to divide 10 roses, 15 sunflowers, and 15 daffodils between two girls.

terms.meaning: 10 identical flowers to be split between two girls
terms.example: Girl 1 can get 0 to 10 roses; 11 possible splits.
terms.connects: Contributes a factor of 11 to the product.

terms.meaning: 15 identical flowers to be split between two girls
terms.example: Girl 1 can get 0 to 15 sunflowers; 16 possible splits.

terms.meaning: 15 identical flowers to be split between two girls
terms.example: Girl 1 can get 0 to 15 daffodils; 16 possible splits.

terms.meaning: Total outcomes = product of independent choices
terms.example: 3 types, each independent, so multiply the counts.
terms.connects: Combines the three factors into the final answer.

given.plan: For each flower type independently count the number of ways girl 1 can receive k flowers (k = 0 to total) and multiply the three counts using the product rule.

steps.title: Count splits for roses
steps.formula_raw: n1 x n2 x ... x nm
steps.apply: Girl 1 can receive 0, 1, 2, ..., 10 roses. That gives 10 + 1 = 11 possible splits.
steps.note: For r identical objects split between 2 people, the number of ways is r + 1.

steps.title: Count splits for sunflowers
steps.formula_raw: n1 x n2 x ... x nm
steps.apply: Girl 1 can receive 0 to 15 sunflowers. That gives 15 + 1 = 16 possible splits.

steps.title: Count splits for daffodils
steps.formula_raw: n1 x n2 x ... x nm
steps.apply: Girl 1 can receive 0 to 15 daffodils. That gives 15 + 1 = 16 possible splits.

steps.title: Apply product rule
steps.note: 2816 does not match any of A (1638), B (2100), or C (2640), so the answer is D.

solution.result: 2816, which is None of the above (option D).

questions.formula_note: The product rule multiplies independent per-type counts to give the total number of distributions.

questions.question:
A multiset is an unordered collection of elements where elements may repeat any number of times. The size of a multiset is the number of elements in it, counting repetitions.

What is the number of multisets of size 4 that can be constructed from n distinct elements so that at least one element occurs exactly twice?
questions.to_find: Number of multisets of size 4 from n distinct elements where at least one element appears exactly twice

understand.plain: A multiset of size 4 from n elements specifies how many times each element appears, where counts sum to 4. We want configurations where at least one element's count is exactly 2. We enumerate two disjoint cases: exactly one element appears twice (and two others appear once each), and exactly two elements each appear twice.

keywords.explain: An unordered collection allowing repeated elements; described by the multiplicity (count) of each element.
keywords.example: {a, a, b, c} is a multiset of size 4 where a appears twice.

keywords.explain: Partitioning the count into complementary cases ensures no overcounting or undercounting.
keywords.example: For size 4 with at least one element appearing exactly twice: either exactly one element appears twice, or exactly two elements each appear twice.

understand.visual_alt: A diagram showing two cases: a multiset with one element doubled and two singletons versus a multiset with two elements each doubled.

given.aim: Count multisets of size 4 drawn from n distinct elements with at least one element having multiplicity exactly 2.

terms.meaning: The sorted list of counts for elements that appear, summing to 4.
terms.example: Pattern (2,1,1) means one element appears twice, two appear once each.

terms.meaning: Two distinct elements each appear exactly twice.
terms.example: {a,a,b,b} from elements {a,b,c,...}.

terms.meaning: One element appears exactly twice, and two distinct other elements appear once each.
terms.example: {a,a,b,c} from {a,b,c,...}.

terms.meaning: Choosing r items from n with repetition allowed (order irrelevant).
terms.example: C(n+r-1, r) counts all such multisets.

given.plan: Enumerate exactly the two patterns (2,2) and (2,1,1) that contain at least one element with multiplicity 2, count each, and add.

steps.title: Identify valid multiplicity patterns
steps.apply: For size 4, the partitions of 4 that include at least one part equal to 2 are: (2,2) and (2,1,1). Patterns (4), (3,1), and (1,1,1,1) have no part equal to 2, so they are excluded.
steps.note: Pattern (2,2): two distinct elements each appear twice. Pattern (2,1,1): one element appears twice, two others appear once.

steps.title: Count pattern (2,2)
steps.apply: Choose 2 elements from n to each appear twice: C(n,2) = n(n-1)/2.
steps.note: The two chosen elements are unordered, hence combination not permutation.

steps.title: Count pattern (2,1,1)
steps.apply: Choose 1 element from n to appear twice (n ways), then choose 2 distinct elements from the remaining n-1 to appear once each: C(n-1, 2) = (n-1)(n-2)/2. Total: n(n-1)(n-2)/2.
steps.note: The element chosen to double and the two singletons must all be distinct.

steps.title: Sum both cases and simplify
steps.note: Factor out n(n-1)/2 from both terms to get the compact closed form.

questions.formula_note: Enumerating the two valid multiplicity patterns and applying the combination formula for each gives the closed-form count.

questions.question:
How many 4-digit even numbers have all digits distinct?

A. 2240
B. 2296
C. 2030
D. 4536
questions.to_find: Count of 4-digit even numbers with all four digits distinct

understand.plain: A 4-digit even number has its last digit from {0, 2, 4, 6, 8} and its first digit from {1, ..., 9} (no leading zero). All four digits must be different. We split into two cases depending on whether the last digit is 0 or a non-zero even digit, because the constraint on the first digit differs between the two cases.

keywords.explain: The thousands digit (d1) cannot be 0; it must be 1 through 9.
For the number to be even, the units digit must be 0, 2, 4, 6, or 8.
keywords.example: 1234 is even because its last digit is 4.

keywords.explain: No digit value may appear more than once across the four positions.
keywords.example: 1234 is valid; 1224 is invalid because 2 repeats.

understand.visual_alt: A 4-position box showing the thousands, hundreds, tens, and units digits with arrows indicating the count of allowed digits for each case.

given.aim: Count all 4-digit even numbers d1 d2 d3 d4 where all di are distinct, d1 != 0, and d4 is even.

terms.meaning: d4 = 0, so d1 can be any of 9 digits (1-9), and d2, d3 fill from the 8 remaining non-zero digits.
d4 is chosen from 4 non-zero even digits, d1 is chosen from {1,...,9} minus {d4} = 8 choices, and d2, d3 fill from remaining 8 digits.
Number of ordered selections of r distinct items from n, equal to n! / (n-r)!.
terms.example: P(8,2) = 56 ordered pairs from 8 elements.
terms.connects: Used to count the middle-position fillings.

terms.meaning: The total count equals the sum of the counts of mutually exclusive cases.
terms.example: Case 1 and Case 2 are disjoint (d4 = 0 vs d4 != 0).

given.plan: Separate into last digit = 0 and last digit in {2,4,6,8}. For each, count using the product rule on the constrained positions, then add the two counts.

steps.title: Case 1 - last digit is 0
steps.apply: Fix d4 = 0. Digit d1 has 9 choices (1-9). Digits d2 and d3 are chosen from the remaining 8 digits: P(8,2) = 56. Count = 9 * 56 = 504.
steps.note: When last digit is 0, the first digit has no restriction other than being non-zero.

steps.title: Case 2 - last digit in {2,4,6,8}
steps.apply: Choose d4: 4 options. Choose d1 from {1,...,9} minus {d4}: 8 options. Choose d2, d3 from the remaining 8 digits: P(8,2) = 56. Count = 4 * 8 * 56 = 1792.
steps.note: d1 cannot equal d4 (distinctness) and cannot be 0 (leading digit constraint), eliminating exactly 2 digits from the 10 for d1.

steps.title: Total by sum rule
steps.note: The two cases are mutually exclusive (d4 = 0 vs d4 != 0) and exhaustive over all even last digits.

questions.formula_note: Splitting on whether the last (even) digit is 0 or non-zero separates the two distinct leading-digit constraints and allows a clean product-rule count for each case.

questions.question:
Let A be a sequence of 8 distinct integers sorted in ascending order. How many distinct pairs of sequences B and C are there such that:
i. each is sorted in ascending order,
ii. B has 3 elements and C has 5 elements, and
iii. the result of merging B and C gives A?

A. 56
B. 2296
C. 2030
D. 256
questions.to_find: Number of ways to split a sorted 8-element sequence into a sorted 3-element sequence B and a sorted 5-element sequence C such that merging them recovers the original sequence

understand.plain: When B and C are merged back into sorted order and must produce A exactly, every element of A must go to either B or C. Because both B and C must be sorted in ascending order and both together must reconstitute A, the split is uniquely determined by which 3 elements from A are assigned to B. Once the 3 elements are chosen, B is those 3 in their original relative order and C is the remaining 5 in theirs — both automatically sorted.

keywords.explain: Merging two sorted sequences produces a sorted sequence; the result is fully determined by which elements came from which source.
The number of ways to choose r items from n without regard to order.
keywords.example: C(8,3) = 56 ways to choose 3 elements from 8.

understand.visual_alt: A sorted array of 8 elements with 3 highlighted positions indicating the chosen elements for B, and the remaining 5 forming C.

given.aim: Count distinct pairs (B, C) where B has 3 and C has 5 elements, both sorted, and merging B and C gives A.

terms.meaning: A sorted sequence of 8 distinct integers, e.g. (a1 < a2 < ... < a8).
terms.connects: The source pool from which B and C draw their elements.

terms.meaning: Any subsequence of a sorted sequence is automatically sorted.
terms.example: Picking elements at positions 1, 4, 7 from a sorted list gives a sorted sub-list.
terms.connects: Guarantees that any 3-element subset of A forms a valid B and the rest forms a valid C.

terms.meaning: Each valid pair (B, C) corresponds uniquely to a 3-element subset of A.
terms.example: Choosing positions {2, 5, 8} from A determines B = (a2, a5, a8) and C = (a1, a3, a4, a6, a7).
terms.connects: Reduces the problem to counting 3-element subsets of an 8-element set.

terms.meaning: The binomial coefficient n! / (r!(n-r)!).

given.plan: Observe that every valid pair (B, C) is determined by which 3 elements of A belong to B; count such subsets using C(8, 3).

steps.title: Establish the bijection
steps.apply: Each valid pair (B, C) is uniquely determined by a 3-element subset S of A: set B to the elements of S in sorted order and C to the remaining 5 elements in sorted order. Merging B and C recovers A. Conversely, every such subset yields a valid pair.
steps.note: The bijection works because the elements are distinct and both B and C inherit the sorted order of A.

steps.title: Count the 3-element subsets
steps.note: This equals C(8,5) = 56 by the symmetry identity, consistent with choosing which 5 elements go to C.

questions.formula_note: The bijection between valid (B, C) pairs and 3-element subsets of A converts the problem directly to a combination count.

questions.question:
n couples are invited to a party with the condition that every husband should be accompanied by his wife. However, a wife need not be accompanied by her husband. The number of different gatherings possible at the party is:

A. 2^(3n) / C(2n, n)
B. 3^n
C. C(2n, n) / 2
D. 2^n * C(2n, n)
questions.to_find: Number of distinct gatherings possible at the party given n couples with the husband-attendance constraint

understand.plain: For each couple, the constraint says a husband can only attend if his wife also attends. This gives exactly three valid attendance states per couple: neither attends, only the wife attends, or both attend. Since the couples are independent of each other, the total number of gatherings is 3 raised to the power n.

keywords.explain: Husband present implies wife present (H => W). The contrapositive is: wife absent implies husband absent.
keywords.example: For couple (H, W): states 'H present, W absent' is forbidden. Allowed: (absent, absent), (absent, present), (present, present).

keywords.explain: When each of n couples independently contributes one of 3 valid states, the total count is 3 * 3 * ... * 3 = 3^n.
keywords.example: With 2 couples, the valid gatherings number 3^2 = 9.

understand.visual_alt: A table with one row per couple showing the three allowed attendance states: (neither), (wife only), (both).

given.aim: Count the number of subsets of the 2n party-goers that satisfy the constraint: for each couple i, husband H_i can be in the subset only if wife W_i is also in the subset.

terms.meaning: The husband attends only if the wife attends; equivalently, wife absent => husband absent.
terms.example: If W_i does not attend, H_i cannot attend either.
terms.connects: Reduces the 4 possible attendance combinations per couple to exactly 3 valid ones.

terms.meaning: State 0: neither attends. State 1: wife only. State 2: both attend.
terms.example: For couple 1 with H1 and W1: {}, {W1}, {H1, W1} are valid; {H1} is not.
terms.connects: Each couple contributes a factor of 3 to the product.

terms.meaning: The choice for each couple is independent of all others.
terms.example: Couple 1 choosing 'wife only' does not restrict couple 2's choice.

terms.meaning: Total outcomes = product of per-couple choices.
terms.example: 3 couples: 3 * 3 * 3 = 27 gatherings.

given.plan: Enumerate the valid attendance states per couple (3 each), note the independence, and apply the product rule.

steps.title: Enumerate states per couple
steps.formula_raw: n1 x n2 x ... x nm
steps.apply: For couple i, list all 4 subsets of {H_i, W_i}: {}, {W_i}, {H_i}, {H_i, W_i}. The state {H_i} (husband without wife) violates the constraint. Removing it leaves 3 valid states: {}, {W_i}, {H_i, W_i}.
steps.note: The forbidden state corresponds to H_i = present AND W_i = absent.

steps.title: Combine across n couples
steps.apply: Each of the n couples independently contributes 3 states. By the product rule, total gatherings = 3 * 3 * ... (n times) = 3^n.
steps.note: This count includes the empty gathering (nobody attends), which is technically valid under the given constraint.

questions.formula_note: Recognizing that each couple independently has exactly 3 valid attendance states converts the problem into a straightforward application of the product rule.

questions.question:
Mala has a colouring book in which each English letter is drawn two times. She wants to paint each of these prints with one of k colours, such that the colour pairs used to colour any two letters are different. Both prints of a letter can also be coloured with the same colour. What is the minimum value of k such that Mala can paint all the letters?

A. 9
B. 8
C. 7
D. 6
questions.to_find: Minimum number of colours k so that each of the 26 letters receives a distinct unordered colour pair

understand.plain: Each letter is painted with two prints, each assigned one of k colours. The pair of colours assigned to a letter is an unordered multiset of size 2 from k colours (since both prints can get the same colour). The total number of distinct such pairs is the number of ways to choose 2 colours with repetition from k, which equals k(k+1)/2. We need k(k+1)/2 >= 26 to give each of the 26 letters a unique pair.

keywords.explain: Since the two prints of a letter can have the same colour and the pair is unordered, we count multisets of size 2 from k colours.
Number of multisets of size r from k elements = C(k+r-1, r). For r=2: C(k+1, 2) = k(k+1)/2.

understand.visual_alt: A grid showing all unordered colour pairs for k colours, with the diagonal representing same-colour pairs and the upper triangle representing different-colour pairs.

given.aim: Find the smallest k such that the number of distinct unordered colour pairs from k colours is at least 26 (one for each English letter).

terms.meaning: Each letter requires a unique unordered colour pair for its two prints.
terms.example: Letter A might get (red, blue); letter B must get a different pair.
terms.connects: Sets the minimum requirement: at least 26 distinct pairs.

terms.term: Colour pair as multiset of size 2
terms.meaning: Each pair is an unordered selection of 2 colours with repetition allowed.
terms.example: (blue, blue) is a valid pair; (blue, red) and (red, blue) are the same pair.
terms.connects: Counted by the formula for combinations with repetition.

terms.meaning: Number of distinct multisets of size r from k elements = C(k+r-1, r).
terms.connects: Gives the number of available distinct colour pairs for k colours.

terms.meaning: We need k(k+1)/2 >= 26.

given.plan: Express the number of available distinct unordered colour pairs as k(k+1)/2, then find the smallest k satisfying k(k+1)/2 >= 26.

steps.title: Count available colour pairs
steps.apply: Each letter gets an unordered pair of colours (repetition allowed, so multiset of size 2). The number of such distinct pairs from k colours is C(k+1, 2) = k(k+1)/2.
steps.note: This equals k choose 2 (different colours) plus k (both prints same colour) = k(k-1)/2 + k = k(k+1)/2.

steps.title: Set up the inequality
steps.apply: We need at least 26 distinct pairs to cover all 26 letters. So k(k+1)/2 >= 26, which means k(k+1) >= 52.
steps.note: Each letter must receive a unique pair; 26 letters require 26 distinct pairs.

steps.title: Find minimum k
steps.note: k=7 gives 28 available pairs, providing 2 extra pairs beyond the 26 needed.

questions.formula_note: The number of unordered colour pairs with repetition from k colours equals k(k+1)/2 via the stars-and-bars (combination with repetition) formula for r=2.

formulas.name: Product Rule
formulas.plain: n1 x n2 x ... x nm
